title: Bank Regulation, CEO Compensation, and Boards
abstract: Abstract We analyze the limits of regulating bank CEO compensation to reduce risk shifting in the presence of an active board that retains the right to approve new investment strategies. Compensation regulation prevents overinvestment in strategies that increase risk, but it is ineffective in preventing underinvestment in strategies that reduce risk. The regulator optimally combines compensation and capital regulations. In contrast, if the board delegates the choice of strategy to the CEO, compensation regulation is sufficient to prevent both types of risk shifting. Compensation regulation increases shareholders’ incentives to implement an active board, which reduces the effectiveness of compensation regulation.
summary_en: Can regulating bank CEO compensation reduce excessive risk-taking — when boards remain free to approve new investment strategies? Kolm, Laux and Lóránth analyse the limits of compensation regulation in this two-tier governance structure. Compensation regulation prevents overinvestment in risk-increasing strategies, but is ineffective in preventing underinvestment in risk-reducing strategies — only one side of the risk-shifting problem is solved. Optimally, the regulator combines compensation regulation with capital requirements. If instead the board fully delegates strategy choice to the CEO, compensation regulation alone suffices for both types of risk shifting. Crucially, compensation regulation itself increases shareholders' incentive to install an active board — undermining the regulation's own effectiveness.
data_used: Theoretical paper. Game-theoretic model of bank governance with active boards, CEO compensation, and risk-shifting incentives.
policy_relevance: For MNB banking-supervision policy and EU CRD regulatory framework: the paper provides theoretical foundation for combining compensation rules with capital requirements rather than relying on either alone. Particularly relevant for the Hungarian banking sector's subsidiary structure where parent-bank boards retain substantial strategy-approval authority. Useful input for the next round of EU bank-governance regulation.
